title: Working with the part that cannot forgive itself for past choices
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that holds the shame of who you used to be

I know this part. I know it so well it feels like an old scar I keep picking at. It's the voice that says "you should have known better" when you look back at who you were five years ago, ten years ago, even yesterday. It's the part that replays every mistake, every moment of cowardice, every time you hurt someone or let yourself down. And it won't let you forget. It won't let you move on. It's convinced that if it stops punishing you, you'll just do it all over again.

Here's the thing. That part isn't your enemy. It's not trying to destroy you, even though it feels that way. It's trying to protect you from ever feeling that kind of pain again. The problem is, it's using shame as a weapon, and shame is a terrible teacher. Shame doesn't help you learn. It just keeps you frozen in the past.

I've sat with clients who can't forgive themselves for things that happened decades ago. Things they did as teenagers, as young adults, as people who were drowning and didn't know how to swim. And I've sat with that same part in myself. The part that still cringes at the memory of a relationship I handled badly, a job I quit in a panic, a promise I broke to someone who trusted me. It's a heavy weight. And it's exhausting.

So let's talk about this part. The one that cannot forgive itself. The one that believes forgiveness is a luxury it doesn't deserve. The one that's stuck in a loop of self-blame and self-hatred. Because I think if we can understand this part, we can start to work with it. And maybe, just maybe, we can find a way to let go.

Where this part comes from and why it's so stubborn

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), talks about parts of us that take on extreme roles to protect the whole system. The part that cannot forgive itself is often what he calls a "firefighter." It's a part that rushes in with intense emotions to distract you from something even more painful underneath. Usually, that deeper pain is a core wound of worthlessness or unlovability.

Think about it. If you can't forgive yourself for something, you're probably holding onto a belief that your mistake proves something fundamental about who you are. That you're bad. That you're broken. That you're not good enough. The unforgiving part keeps you focused on the mistake so you don't have to feel the terror of being flawed. It's a terrible deal, but it's a deal your system made when you didn't have better options.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, would say this part is stuck in a trauma response. When you did something that hurt yourself or someone else, your nervous system may have gone into freeze or collapse. The shame and self-blame are the body's way of immobilizing you, keeping you small, keeping you safe from the threat of rejection or abandonment. The part that won't forgive is actually a survival mechanism. It's trying to keep you from making the same mistake again by making you feel so bad that you never repeat it.

But here's the problem. That strategy worked when you were younger, when you had less capacity, when you didn't have the tools to process the shame differently. Now it's just a prison. And the part that holds the keys doesn't know how to let you out. It's stuck in an old role, doing an old job, and it doesn't realize that you're not the same person anymore.

I remember a client who couldn't forgive herself for leaving an abusive relationship too late. She was furious at herself for staying for years, for making excuses, for not protecting her children sooner. That unforgiving part was loud and relentless. But when we sat with it, we found a terrified younger part underneath. A part that believed if she left sooner, she would have failed at being a good wife, a good mother, a good person. The unforgiving part was actually protecting her from that deeper shame. It was a shield, not a sword.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. She talks about how we can meet our most hated parts with compassion instead of more judgment. It's not about condoning what happened. It's about acknowledging that the part that can't forgive is suffering too. And that suffering deserves care.

How to talk to the part that won't let go

If you try to force forgiveness, the part will only dig in deeper. It will scream louder. It will point to more evidence of why you don't deserve to move on. So don't try to force anything. Instead, try curiosity.

Find a quiet moment. Sit down, close your eyes, and take a few breaths. Notice where the unforgiving part lives in your body. For me, it's a tightness in my chest and a knot in my stomach. For you, it might be a weight on your shoulders or a clenching in your jaw. Just notice it. Don't try to change it.

Then, ask it a question. Not from a place of frustration, but from genuine interest. Something like, "What are you afraid would happen if I forgave myself?" or "What job are you trying to do for me?" Stay with me here, because the answer might surprise you. The part might say something like, "If you forgive yourself, you'll forget what you did and do it again." Or, "If you forgive yourself, you'll be letting yourself off the hook, and you don't deserve that." Or, "If you forgive yourself, you'll have to feel the grief of what you lost."

Listen to the part. Don't argue with it. Just let it speak. You might feel a lot of emotion come up. That's okay. That's the part finally being heard. Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who writes about parts work, says that parts often soften when they feel seen. They don't need to be fixed. They need to be understood.

Once the part has spoken, you can thank it. You can say, "I see you're trying to protect me. I appreciate that. I know you've been doing this for a long time, and it's been hard work." That doesn't mean you agree with the part's strategy. It just means you're acknowledging its effort. And that alone can start to shift something.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It walks you through how to relate to your inner system with curiosity rather than judgment. It's a good companion for this kind of work.

Forgiveness as a process, not a decision

I used to think forgiveness was a switch you flipped. You decide to forgive, and then you're done. But that's not how it works with the parts that carry deep shame. Forgiveness is more like a slow unwinding. It's a conversation that happens over days, weeks, months. The unforgiving part doesn't just vanish. It gradually learns to trust that you're safe now, that you have new resources, that you won't repeat the past.

One of the most helpful things I've learned from Pete Walker's work on C-PTSD is the idea of "reparenting" the parts of yourself that got stuck in childhood or adolescence. The part that can't forgive is often a young part that didn't have an adult around to help it process shame. It needed someone to say, "You made a mistake, but you're still good. You're still lovable. You're still worthy of connection." If you didn't get that, the part is still waiting for it.

So you become that adult now. You sit with the part and offer the reassurance it never got. You say, "I know you're scared. I know you think you're bad. But I'm here now, and I can handle this. You don't have to carry this alone anymore." It sounds simple, but it's not easy. The part will resist. It will test you. It will bring up more memories, more evidence, more reasons why you should keep punishing yourself. That's okay. Just keep showing up.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He breaks down the inner critic and the shame cycle in a way that makes it feel less overwhelming. It's a book I've recommended to many people who are stuck in self-blame.

What happens when the part starts to loosen its grip

You might notice small shifts at first. A moment where you think about the past and don't feel that familiar twist in your gut. A day where the voice of self-blame is quieter. A dream where you're not running from something. These are signs that the part is starting to trust you. It's letting go of its old role.

But don't expect a clean break. The part will probably come back when you're stressed or tired or triggered. That's normal. That's how parts work. They don't disappear. They just become less extreme. They start to relax into a more balanced role. The part that once screamed "you're unforgivable" might become a part that gently reminds you to learn from your mistakes. It shifts from a tyrant to a teacher.

And here's the strange thing. When that part softens, you might feel grief. Grief for the years you spent punishing yourself. Grief for the relationships you damaged while you were stuck in shame. Grief for the person you were who didn't know any better. Let that grief come. It's part of the healing. It's the price of letting go.

I won't tell you that forgiveness is the only path. Some people never fully forgive themselves, and they still find a way to live with meaning. But I will say this. The part that cannot forgive is not your enemy. It's a part that's been doing a job it never asked for, carrying a weight it was never meant to hold. It deserves your compassion, not your contempt. And when you start to give it that, something shifts. Not overnight. Not perfectly. But it shifts.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because sometimes the part that can't forgive also needs help setting boundaries with the people who remind you of your past mistakes. It's all connected.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker
